The real economics of AI adoption live in the gap between the pitch and the payoff. Most buying decisions never look there.
TLDR; Get the tool here: https://getnali.com/tools/ai-adoption-roi
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The headline number and the number you actually keep are rarely the same.A finance director sits with a vendor’s one-pager. The math is clean. The task takes twenty minutes by hand, the team does a thousand a month, and the tool automates most of it. Multiply it out and the slide says what every slide like it says: roughly thirty thousand dollars a month, saved.
It is a real number. It is also wrong — not because anyone lied, but because it answers a question nobody should ask. The honest figure, the one that shows up in the budget twelve months later, is closer to eleven thousand. The gap between those two numbers is where every AI buying decision is actually won or lost, and almost nobody looks there before signing.
This isn’t an argument against adopting AI; plenty of these projects pay off handsomely. It’s an argument for doing the arithmetic the pitch skips — because the businesses that get burned and the ones that get rich are often automating the same workflow. The only difference is whether they understood the numbers underneath the headline.
The seductive number
The pitch always reduces to one calculation:
Time saved × What time costs.
It’s seductive because it’s easy, and because the buyer already half-believes it.
Anyone who has spent years inside these projects learns to distrust it on sight — not because it’s fabricated, but because it assumes the best case on every variable at once. It assumes the

• machine takes the whole task

• does it perfectly

• the moment it’s switched on, and

• that the people it replaces were free.
None of those four things is true. And each one is a number of its own.
How much of the work can actually leave?
Start with the most flattering assumption: that AI does the whole task.
It rarely does. A workflow that looks like one job is usually three or four, and the machine is genuinely good at one of them. Document extraction is a clean example — modern systems read an invoice with startling accuracy. But “process the invoice” was never just reading it; it was judging the odd exception, chasing the supplier when something didn’t reconcile, deciding what to do when a number looked wrong. The reading leaves. The judgment stays.
So the first real number isn’t “what does this task cost” but “what share of it can credibly leave the building.” For some workflows that share is large. For anything where the hard part is judgment rather than transcription, it’s smaller than the demo suggests — and get it wrong, and every number downstream is inflated, because you’ve priced the saving on work that was never going anywhere.
The machine makes its own mistakes — and you have to measure them honestly
Here’s where the most common trap lives.
Picture a mid-sized professional-services firm — a brokerage — that automated the drafting of client-facing summaries. The pitch was textbook: twenty minutes each, high volume, enormous savings. The drafts came back fast and read well. Then the trouble started, quietly, the way it always does.
Roughly one draft in ten carried a confident error — a transposed figure, a misstated term, a clause that didn’t apply. Not gibberish; the dangerous kind. The outputs that read most fluently were sometimes the ones that were wrong, because fluency and accuracy aren’t the same thing, and a machine that has learned to sound certain sounds certain even when it has invented the detail. A few reached clients before anyone noticed.
The instinct is to subtract the AI’s error rate from the savings and move on. But that’s the wrong comparison — and getting it wrong is what makes most of these cases dishonest in both directions. The person doing the task today isn’t flawless either. They have an error rate; those mistakes already happen and already cost money. The number that matters isn’t how often the AI errs, but whether it errs more or less than the human it replaced, after a reviewer has looked.
That correction changes everything. For clean, structured work, a good system plus a quick review often makes fewer errors than a tired person at 4pm — and the honest case is better than the pitch. For fluent, generative work at high stakes, the machine introduces errors faster than the human ever did, and the case is far worse. You can’t know which you’re looking at until you measure both sides. The pitch only shows you one.
Someone has to check the work
Return to the brokerage, because the part that finished their story is the part nobody budgets for.
Once a few bad drafts reached clients, the firm had everything reviewed before it went out. Correct — and expensive in a way the original sum never counted. The juniors who used to write the summaries were cheap. The seniors now checking them were not. The firm had swapped a junior writing task for a senior reviewing task, kept most of the volume, and added a cost the calculation had treated as zero.
This is the overhead that turns green projects amber. Oversight isn’t free, it doesn’t catch everything even when it happens, and the more dangerous the mistake, the more of it you need. Cosmetic errors run with a light touch; an error that reaches a client, a patient, or a regulator demands heavy review — paid for in exactly the expensive hours the automation was meant to give back.
The brokerage’s thirty-thousand-dollar saving was real, in that the drafting time genuinely shrank. It was also almost entirely consumed by review labour and a couple of errors that cost more than a year of the tool. The truer number was small, and on one workflow it was negative. Nobody had done that subtraction before buying.
It does not arrive on the day you switch it on
Even when the economics are sound, the calendar lies.
Savings are quoted as if they begin on day one and run flat forever. Real adoption has a shape: an integration period, a stretch where the team is learning the new way and is slower than before, and a ramp where the benefit climbs to its full level over months, not days. The honest version of any twelve-month figure has a dip near the start — a real, payable cost of the transition — before it ever turns positive.
This is where businesses misjudge the buy the other way. The brokerage overpaid by ignoring costs. The next one underestimates the time.
The value is often not where you are looking
A regional dental group automated its front desk expecting one thing: fewer reception hours. That was the number on the slide.
The staff-time saving turned out modest and slow. The complex calls still needed a person; the simple ones were a smaller share of the day than assumed; and the team took a full quarter to trust the system. Against the pitch, the first months looked like a disappointment.
But something else was happening. The same system was quietly confirming and re-booking the appointments most likely to be missed — and no-shows, in a practice like that, are pure lost revenue. Within a couple of months the recovered revenue from filled chairs dwarfed every dollar of labour the project had been sold on. The investment paid off handsomely — in a currency nobody had been tracking, on a line item that wasn’t on the slide.
The lesson isn’t “AI surprises you.” It’s that the headline metric — hours saved — is frequently the wrong thing to measure, and the real return hides in recovered revenue, avoided loss, or capacity redeployed to work that grows the business. Boards have noticed; “hours saved” has quietly stopped counting as a result on its own, because the hours so often evaporated into other meetings. The businesses that win measure the right thing, not the obvious thing.
And the cost you cannot put on a spreadsheet
One more variable, and it should govern the whole decision: what does a mistake actually cost here?
A wrong word in a marketing caption is an embarrassment. A wrong dose in a pharmacy, a wrong figure in financial advice, a missed conflict in a legal file — these aren’t the same category of event, and no efficiency saving is worth pretending they are. The entire risk calculation bends around this question, and a generic ROI sum never asks it.
This is where the only real fail-safe lives. For high-stakes work, the answer isn’t “automate carefully.” It’s “keep a human in command, by design, and accept that you’re buying a faster assistant, not a replacement decision-maker.” The most honest thing a tool can tell you is that for this workflow, the responsible setup has the machine draft and a qualified person decide — and the economics must be judged on that basis, not on a fantasy of full automation no one should deploy.
A buying process that lets you talk yourself out of that fail-safe to make the number look better isn’t saving you money. It’s selling you a liability at a discount.
It was never a yes-or-no question
Step back and the real shape appears. “Should we adopt AI” is the wrong question; it has no answer. The right one is asked one workflow at a time, and the answers fall into two honest buckets.
Some workflows are strong candidates — the share of the work that can leave is large, errors are cheap, oversight is light, the value is real and reasonably quick.
Prioritise them. Others aren’t yet a confident yes — thin time-saving, expensive review, a costly mistake, or a slow ramp leave the case too close to call on a back-of-envelope basis. That second bucket isn’t a “no.” It’s a “look closer before you commit, financially and otherwise.”
A business that sorts its workflows into those two buckets before it buys has already done more diligence than most do after. It knows where to move fast and where to slow down — and it has stopped making one company-wide bet on a question that was always going to be answered task by task.
A way to do the arithmetic
Doing this properly used to mean sitting down with someone who’d seen enough of these projects to know which numbers to distrust. The reasoning isn’t complicated, but it has a lot of moving parts — the offloadable share, the two error rates, the oversight overhead, the ramp, the fully-loaded cost of the people involved, and the price of a mistake in your specific corner of the world.
We built a free tool that runs that arithmetic by industry and workflow, with research-backed defaults for the numbers you can’t be expected to know off-hand — and it’s honest about which figures are solid and which are estimates worth replacing with your own. It won’t make the decision for you. It’ll tell you, workflow by workflow, which ones are strong candidates and which deserve a closer look before you sign.
